THE ORANGE MINSTREL.

The Orange Institution, since its inception over a century ago, hag
played a prominent part in the social and political life of Ireland, from
the Causeway to Cork. Recruited in the main, from the artisan and
shopkeeping classes, its leaders have been great landed proprietors;
the offshoots of the aristocracy, and churchmen, mostly gradumates of

- Trinity, men bought up in a literary and cultured atmosphere. When

one considers these origins it is surprising how little has been added
{o the common stock of Anglo-Irish literature by Orange writers. One
searches in vain through the various anthologies—a fair test—for any
representative selections from their writings. There is one notable
exception—Col. Blacker, who, by the excellence of a few lyrics, is by
general acclaim, considered most worthy of the title of ‘‘ The Orange
Minstrel.”” Though he loomed large in the public life of his day, and
wag considered worthy a niche in the gallery of celebrities in the old
“Dublin University Magazino’’—from which we take his portrait—
yet the chief works of reference are silent regarding him, and his fame
survives solely through two or three ballads too good to die. 4

~ William Blacker, the son of Dean Blacker (descended from a ]?gqn-
ish Blacar), and Eliza, daughter of Sir Hugh Hill, M.P. for Derry, wae

born‘on 1st September, 1777, at Carrickblacker, Co. ‘Armagh. In this .

fine old mansion on the banks of the Bann, erected ghortly after the
Revolution, and filled with relics of that stirring time, such as the
eaddle-cloth used by William III. at the Boyne, Ia-nd some stone can-
non balls fired in defence of Derry, the boy grew up imbibing those

 ideas of loyalty and ascendancy whioh inspired his whole career. ' Edu-

cated at the Royal School of Armagh, under his “ lamented Carpen-

dale,”” he passed on to T.C.D. in 1796, where he had, as fellow students,

Tom Moore, the Emmets, Wilson Croker, and others, afterwards dii-s-:
tinguished, The famous Visitation of Lord Clare to the College 1n
1798 to inquire into the existence of treasonablo opinions a..mong-st the
8laff and gtudents, which resulted in the expulsion of Whitley Stokes

[ ' JRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On Saturday, 8th March, Miss Ethel Rolt Wheeler delivered a lec-
ture entitled ‘‘ Romance in Irish Poetry and Drama.” Mr. W. B. Yeats
occupied the chair. The lecturer said that in looking back into the
past she saw four types of women that have inspired the great love

stories of Irish: myth and saga. Towering above all, vague in a kind

ARTEUR LAw, the well-known dramatic author, died at Parkstone,
Dorset, on 2nd April. He was born at Killaloe, of which place his
father was rector, and his grandfather (Arbuthnot) bishop, on 22nd
March, 1844. He entered the Army, served for eight years, retiring
with the rank of lieutenant, and took to the stage. He was a prolific
producer of plays, 67 in all, 48 of which are enumerated in * Who's
.Who,”’ the most successful being ‘‘ The New Boy.”” He was, sags ‘‘The
Times,”” * a delightful and unassuming personality.”’

EArL oF BeLMORE, G.C.M.G., who died on 6thi April, at his
residence, Castle Coole, Enniskillen, was the fourth earl, an Irish re-
presemtative peer, and an extensive landowner in Ulster, where he was
very popular. Born on Oth April, 1835, he was educated at Eton and
Trmity College, Cambridge. He was Under Secretary for Home Affairs,
1866-67, and Governor of New South Wales 1868-72. On his return

and published the following works :—Parliamentary Memoirs of Tyrone
and Iermanagh, 8vo., 1887; Two Ulster Manors, Finagh and Coole,
8vo., 1881, 2nd enlarged ed., 1903 ; History of the Corry Family, 1891.

torical Atlas; Old Castles of Co. Tyrone; James Spottiswoode, Bishop

Lorp Dunsany makes a strange figure in contemporary literature.
An Irishman, he comes of a land richly stocked w ith legend which hag
already fired the imagination of great poets and dreamers. With this
common heritage it would have been but natural to find him adventur--
ing on the same paths as his fellow countryman W. B. Yeats, for both

seek to see behind the veils which limit everyday vision, turning with

indifference from realities which are only the duller part of a universal -

illusion, to the contemplation of the eternal.  But while Yeats has
stayed in his beloved Ireland, Dunsany seems to have tasted of some
magical Eastern herb which has taken his mind captive and imprisoned
it in odorous realms of an Orient more enchanting, more wonderfully
wrought than any told of by earthly travellers.—T.P.’s Weekly.

The idea that an author keeps by him every bit of writing he has
ever done is a delusion, and he sometimes has real difficulty in getting
a copy of an early book by himself. Recently Mr. George Moore wanted
a copy of a volume of Impressions which he wrote some twenty years
ago, 1n order to revise it for a new edition which Mr. Werner Laurie
is publishing. He could not get one at all for a long time, and then
it came to him in an ¢dd way and it cost him £3 10s. That was a case
where it would have paid to keep at least one copy of the book for
himself, but their kind friends have a habit of going away with author’s
copies.—The Book Monthly.
